title: Customer Loyalty Analysis
desc: Find customers who made >5 purchases but are NOT enrolled in the loyalty program.
sql:
SELECT
    c.CustomerID,
    c.FirstName || ' ' || c.LastName AS customer_name,
    c.Email,
    c.Phone,
    COUNT(o.OrderID) AS total_purchases
FROM Customer c
JOIN "Order" o ON o.CustomerID = c.CustomerID
WHERE c.CustomerID NOT IN (
    SELECT CustomerID FROM LoyaltyProgram
)
AND o.Status IN ('Completed','Delivered')
GROUP BY c.CustomerID, c.FirstName, c.LastName, c.Email, c.Phone
HAVING COUNT(o.OrderID) > 5
ORDER BY total_purchases DESC;

notes.title: What does NOT IN (subquery) do?
notes.text: In this bakery context, nOT IN checks that c.CustomerID does not appear in the result set returned by the subquery. The subquery SELECT CustomerID FROM LoyaltyProgram returns all enrolled customers. So NOT IN filters down to only customers who have never enrolled.

notes.title: Why is HAVING used instead of WHERE?
notes.text: In this bakery context, wHERE filters individual rows before grouping. HAVING filters after aggregation. Since we need to count orders first and then keep only groups with count > 5, we must use HAVING COUNT(o.OrderID) > 5. Writing this condition in WHERE would cause a syntax error because aggregate functions cannot appear in WHERE.

notes.title: Why GROUP BY lists every non-aggregated column
notes.text: In this bakery context, sQL requires that every column in SELECT that is not inside an aggregate function (COUNT, SUM, etc.) must appear in GROUP BY. That is why c.FirstName, c.LastName, c.Email, and c.Phone are all listed.

notes.title: What does the WHERE clause on Status do?
notes.text: In this bakery context, the condition o.Status IN ('Completed','Delivered') ensures we only count orders that were actually fulfilled. Cancelled or pending orders are excluded so the purchase count is accurate.

checklist: NOT IN with subquery to exclude loyalty members
businessMeaning: The bakery wants to know which repeat buyers have not joined loyalty so staff can target signups.
expectedResultShape: Columns returned are based on the SELECT list for Customer Loyalty Analysis.
One row per customer not enrolled in loyalty, with purchase count.
oralQuestions: In one sentence, what does Customer Loyalty Analysis tell the bakery owner?
Why is each JOIN required for this requirement?
What would change if the WHERE filters were removed?
Why is GROUP BY or HAVING needed here (if present)?

title: Inventory Shortage Alert
desc: Identify ingredients that are below the minimum stock level.
sql:
SELECT
    i.IngredientName,
    inv.CurrentStock AS current_stock,
    inv.ReorderThreshold AS minimum_stock,
    i.UnitOfMeasure AS unit,
    ROUND((inv.ReorderThreshold - inv.CurrentStock)::numeric, 2) AS shortage
FROM Inventory inv
JOIN Ingredient i ON i.IngredientID = inv.IngredientID
WHERE inv.CurrentStock < inv.ReorderThreshold
ORDER BY shortage DESC;

notes.title: What does the WHERE clause do here?
notes.text: In this bakery context, wHERE inv.CurrentStock < inv.ReorderThreshold compares two columns in the same row. It keeps only rows where the current stock has fallen below the reorder threshold — these are the ingredients that need restocking.

notes.title: What does ::numeric mean?
notes.text: In this bakery context, the ::numeric is a PostgreSQL type cast. It explicitly converts the value to type numeric so ROUND() can format it to 2 decimal places. Without the cast, ROUND may throw an error on non-numeric types.

notes.title: Column aliasing with AS
notes.text: In this bakery context, aS current_stock, AS minimum_stock, and AS shortage rename the output columns to human-readable labels. The alias shortage is a computed column created from an arithmetic expression, not a stored column in any table.

notes.title: Why ORDER BY shortage DESC?
notes.text: In this bakery context, sorting by shortage DESC puts the most critically low ingredients at the top. This makes the report actionable — the bakery operations team sees the most urgent bakery items first.

checklist: WHERE CurrentStock < ReorderThreshold compares two columns
ORDER BY shortage DESC for priority view
businessMeaning: The bakery wants to know which ingredients are below reorder level before production is interrupted.
expectedResultShape: Columns returned are based on the SELECT list for Inventory Shortage Alert.
One row per ingredient below threshold, ordered by largest shortage first.
oralQuestions: In one sentence, what does Inventory Shortage Alert tell the bakery owner?
Why is each JOIN required for this requirement?
What would change if the WHERE filters were removed?

title: Top Employee Performance
desc: Find the employee who processed the most orders last month.
sql:
SELECT
    e.EmployeeID,
    e.FirstName || ' ' || e.LastName AS employee_name,
    r.RoleName,
    COUNT(oa.OrderID) AS order_count
FROM OrderAssignment oa
JOIN Employee e ON e.EmployeeID = oa.EmployeeID
JOIN Role r ON r.RoleID = e.RoleID
JOIN "Order" o ON o.OrderID = oa.OrderID
WHERE o.OrderDate >= DATE_TRUNC('month', CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '1 month')
  AND o.OrderDate <  DATE_TRUNC('month', CURRENT_DATE)
GROUP BY e.EmployeeID, e.FirstName, e.LastName, r.RoleName
ORDER BY order_count DESC
LIMIT 5;

notes.title: How does DATE_TRUNC work to get 'last month'?
notes.text: In this bakery context, dATE_TRUNC('month', CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '1 month') returns the first day of the previous month at midnight. Combined with < DATE_TRUNC('month', CURRENT_DATE) (first day of current month), this creates a precise range covering exactly last month — regardless of how many days it has.

notes.title: Why are there multiple JOINs?
notes.text: In this bakery context, data is spread across normalized tables. OrderAssignment links orders to employees, Employee holds names, Role holds job titles, and Order holds dates. JOINs reconnect these related tables so we can filter by date while displaying employee names and roles.

notes.title: What does LIMIT 5 do?
notes.text: In this bakery context, lIMIT 5 restricts the output to the top 5 rows after sorting. Combined with ORDER BY order_count DESC, it returns only the top 5 performers. Without ORDER BY, LIMIT would return an arbitrary 5 rows.

notes.title: Why "Order" is in double quotes
notes.text: In this bakery context, oRDER is a reserved keyword in SQL (used in ORDER BY). The table was named "Order" with double quotes to escape the reserved word. Every reference must use "Order" with quotes, or queries will fail.

checklist: Chain of 4 JOINs: OrderAssignment → Employee → Role → Order
Half-open date range: >= start AND < end
ORDER BY order_count DESC LIMIT 5 for top-N pattern
businessMeaning: The bakery wants to know which team members handled the most orders last month for performance review.
expectedResultShape: Columns returned are based on the SELECT list for Top Employee Performance.
One row per employee for last month, ranked by handled orders.
oralQuestions: In one sentence, what does Top Employee Performance tell the bakery owner?
Why is each JOIN required for this requirement?

title: Supplier Expense Report
desc: Calculate total expenses for each supplier in the last quarter.
sql:
SELECT
    s.SupplierName,
    s.ContactName,
    COUNT(inv.InvoiceID) AS invoice_count,
    SUM(inv.TotalAmount) AS total_expense
FROM Invoice inv
JOIN Supplier s ON s.SupplierID = inv.SupplierID
WHERE inv.InvoiceDate >= DATE_TRUNC('quarter', CURRENT_DATE)
  AND inv.InvoiceDate <  DATE_TRUNC('quarter', CURRENT_DATE) + INTERVAL '3 months'
GROUP BY s.SupplierID, s.SupplierName, s.ContactName
ORDER BY total_expense DESC;

notes.title: What is the effect of GROUP BY here?
notes.text: In this bakery context, gROUP BY s.SupplierID, s.SupplierName, s.ContactName collapses all invoice rows for the same supplier into a single output row. COUNT and SUM compute totals within each group. Without GROUP BY, we would get one grand total for all suppliers combined.

notes.title: How does DATE_TRUNC('quarter', ...) work?
notes.text: In this bakery context, dATE_TRUNC('quarter', CURRENT_DATE) returns the first day of the current quarter (Jan 1, Apr 1, Jul 1, or Oct 1). Adding INTERVAL '3 months' gives the start of the next quarter. Together they form an inclusive-start / exclusive-end range that captures exactly one quarter.

notes.title: COUNT vs SUM
notes.text: In this bakery context, cOUNT(inv.InvoiceID) counts how many invoice records exist per supplier. SUM(inv.TotalAmount) adds up the dollar values. They answer different questions: 'how many invoices?' vs. 'how much money total?'

notes.title: Why include SupplierID in GROUP BY?
notes.text: In this bakery context, two suppliers could theoretically share the same name. Grouping by s.SupplierID (the primary key) guarantees each group represents exactly one supplier.

checklist: INTERVAL '3 months' to get quarter end
businessMeaning: The bakery wants to know supplier spend by quarter to control purchasing costs.
expectedResultShape: Columns returned are based on the SELECT list for Supplier Expense Report.
One row per supplier with invoice count and total spend for the quarter.
oralQuestions: In one sentence, what does Supplier Expense Report tell the bakery owner?
Why is each JOIN required for this requirement?
What would change if the WHERE filters were removed?
Why is GROUP BY or HAVING needed here (if present)?
What does one output row represent in business terms?

title: Product Review Insights
desc: Identify the top 3 highest-rated and top 3 lowest-rated products.
sql:
(
  SELECT
      p.ProductName,
      ROUND(AVG(rt.Score)::numeric, 2) AS avg_rating,
      COUNT(rv.ReviewID) AS review_count,
      'Top Rated' AS category
  FROM Review rv
  JOIN Product p ON p.ProductID = rv.ProductID
  JOIN Rating rt ON rt.RatingID = rv.RatingID
  GROUP BY p.ProductID, p.ProductName
  HAVING COUNT(rv.ReviewID) >= 2
  ORDER BY avg_rating DESC, review_count DESC
  LIMIT 3
)
UNION ALL
(
  SELECT
      p.ProductName,
      ROUND(AVG(rt.Score)::numeric, 2) AS avg_rating,
      COUNT(rv.ReviewID) AS review_count,
      'Lowest Rated' AS category
  FROM Review rv
  JOIN Product p ON p.ProductID = rv.ProductID
  JOIN Rating rt ON rt.RatingID = rv.RatingID
  GROUP BY p.ProductID, p.ProductName
  HAVING COUNT(rv.ReviewID) >= 2
  ORDER BY avg_rating ASC, review_count DESC
  LIMIT 3
);

notes.title: What does UNION ALL do?
notes.text: In this bakery context, uNION ALL stacks the results of two SELECT statements vertically. The first finds the top 3 highest-rated; the second finds the top 3 lowest-rated. UNION ALL keeps all rows. Using plain UNION instead would remove duplicates, which is unnecessary here and slower.

notes.title: Why is HAVING COUNT >= 2 used?
notes.text: In this bakery context, hAVING COUNT(rv.ReviewID) >= 2 filters out bakery products with only one review. A single review is not statistically meaningful. This ensures the averages are based on at least some consensus.

notes.title: Literal column: 'Top Rated' AS category
notes.text: In this bakery context, writing 'Top Rated' AS category creates a constant text column in every row. This labels each row so after UNION ALL, you can tell which half of the result a row came from.

notes.title: Why parentheses around each SELECT?
notes.text: In this bakery context, in PostgreSQL, when using ORDER BY and LIMIT inside a UNION ALL, each subquery must be wrapped in parentheses. Without them, the database cannot determine which query the ORDER BY belongs to.

checklist: Two SELECTs joined with UNION ALL (not UNION)
Each SELECT wrapped in parentheses for ORDER BY + LIMIT to work
HAVING COUNT >= 2 to require minimum review sample
Literal 'Top Rated' / 'Lowest Rated' column to label rows
First query: ORDER BY avg_rating DESC LIMIT 3
Second query: ORDER BY avg_rating ASC LIMIT 3
businessMeaning: The bakery wants to know top and lowest rated bakery products to improve menu decisions.
expectedResultShape: Columns returned are based on the SELECT list for Product Review Insights.
Up to six rows: top 3 and lowest 3 products by average rating.
oralQuestions: In one sentence, what does Product Review Insights tell the bakery owner?
Why is each JOIN required for this requirement?
What would change if the WHERE filters were removed?

What does one output row represent in business terms?

title: Promotion Effectiveness
desc: Compare sales before and during promotion campaigns.
sql:
WITH promo_products AS (
    SELECT
        pp.ProductID,
        p.ProductName,
        pr.PromotionName,
        pr.StartDate AS promo_start,
        pr.EndDate AS promo_end,
        pr.StartDate - INTERVAL '30 days' AS before_start,
        pr.StartDate - INTERVAL '1 day' AS before_end
    FROM ProductPromotion pp
    JOIN Product p ON p.ProductID = pp.ProductID
    JOIN Promotion pr ON pr.PromotionID = pp.PromotionID
    WHERE pr.StartDate >= '2026-01-01'
),
before_sales AS (
    SELECT pp.ProductID, pp.PromotionName,
           COALESCE(SUM(ol.Quantity), 0) AS qty_before
    FROM promo_products pp
    LEFT JOIN OrderLine ol ON ol.ProductID = pp.ProductID
    LEFT JOIN "Order" o ON o.OrderID = ol.OrderID
        AND o.OrderDate::date BETWEEN pp.before_start AND pp.before_end
        AND o.Status IN ('Completed','Delivered')
    GROUP BY pp.ProductID, pp.PromotionName
),
during_sales AS (
    SELECT pp.ProductID, pp.PromotionName,
           COALESCE(SUM(ol.Quantity), 0) AS qty_during
    FROM promo_products pp
    LEFT JOIN OrderLine ol ON ol.ProductID = pp.ProductID
    LEFT JOIN "Order" o ON o.OrderID = ol.OrderID
        AND o.OrderDate::date BETWEEN pp.promo_start AND pp.promo_end
        AND o.Status IN ('Completed','Delivered')
    GROUP BY pp.ProductID, pp.PromotionName
)
SELECT
    pp.ProductName,
    pp.PromotionName,
    bs.qty_before AS sales_before,
    ds.qty_during AS sales_during,
    CASE WHEN bs.qty_before > 0
         THEN ROUND(((ds.qty_during - bs.qty_before)::numeric / bs.qty_before) * 100, 1)
         ELSE NULL
    END AS growth_rate_pct
FROM promo_products pp
JOIN before_sales bs ON bs.ProductID = pp.ProductID AND bs.PromotionName = pp.PromotionName
JOIN during_sales ds ON ds.ProductID = pp.ProductID AND ds.PromotionName = pp.PromotionName
ORDER BY pp.PromotionName, pp.ProductName;

notes.title: What is a CTE (WITH clause)?
notes.text: In this bakery context, a Common Table Expression (WITH ... AS) defines a temporary named result set that can be referenced later in the same query. Here, three CTEs are chained: promo_bakery products identifies what to compare, before_sales counts pre-promo quantities, and during_sales counts promo-period quantities. CTEs make complex queries readable and modular.

notes.title: Why use COALESCE(SUM(...), 0)?
notes.text: In this bakery context, if a bakery product had zero sales in a period, the LEFT JOIN produces no matching rows, and SUM returns NULL. COALESCE(..., 0) converts that to 0, preventing NULLs from breaking the growth rate calculation.

notes.title: LEFT JOIN vs JOIN here
notes.text: In this bakery context, lEFT JOIN is used for the sales subqueries because a bakery product might have zero sales before or during the promo. A regular JOIN would drop those rows entirely, making it look like the bakery product does not exist rather than showing '0 sales.'

notes.title: CASE WHEN for safe division
notes.text: In this bakery context, the CASE WHEN bs.qty_before > 0 prevents division by zero. If no sales occurred before the promotion, growth rate is mathematically undefined, so we return NULL instead of crashing.

checklist: LEFT JOIN so zero-sales products still appear
CASE WHEN > 0 prevents division by zero
Final SELECT JOINs all 3 CTEs together
businessMeaning: The bakery wants to know whether promotions increased sales versus the pre-promo baseline.
expectedResultShape: Columns returned are based on the SELECT list for Promotion Effectiveness.
One row per product-promotion pair with before vs during quantity and growth percent.
oralQuestions: In one sentence, what does Promotion Effectiveness tell the bakery owner?
Why is each JOIN required for this requirement?
What would change if the WHERE filters were removed?
Why is GROUP BY or HAVING needed here (if present)?
What does one output row represent in business terms?

title: Catering Revenue
desc: Summarize total catering revenue by event type.
sql:
SELECT
    et.TypeName AS event_type,
    COUNT(c.CateringID) AS number_of_orders,
    SUM(c.TotalCost) AS total_revenue,
    ROUND(AVG(c.TotalCost)::numeric, 2) AS avg_revenue_per_event
FROM Catering c
JOIN Event e ON e.EventID = c.EventID
JOIN EventType et ON et.EventTypeID = e.EventTypeID
GROUP BY et.EventTypeID, et.TypeName
ORDER BY total_revenue DESC;

notes.title: How do the JOINs chain together?
notes.text: In this bakery context, catering → Event → EventType is a chain following foreign keys. Each catering record links to an event, and each event has a type. The two JOINs 'walk' this chain to reach TypeName so we can group and label by event type.

notes.title: What is the effect of GROUP BY here?
notes.text: In this bakery context, gROUP BY et.EventTypeID, et.TypeName collapses all catering records sharing the same event type into one row per type. COUNT tells how many events, SUM gives total revenue, and AVG gives the average — all within each group.

notes.title: Multiple aggregates in one query
notes.text: In this bakery context, you can use several aggregate functions (COUNT, SUM, AVG) together in the same SELECT. Each one operates independently on the rows within each group defined by GROUP BY.

checklist: Chain of 2 JOINs: Catering → Event → EventType
businessMeaning: The bakery wants to know which catering event types generate the strongest revenue.
expectedResultShape: Columns returned are based on the SELECT list for Catering Revenue.
One row per event type with total and average catering revenue.
oralQuestions: In one sentence, what does Catering Revenue tell the bakery owner?
Why is each JOIN required for this requirement?

title: Nutritional & Allergy Compliance
desc: List products missing nutritional or allergy information.
sql:
SELECT
    p.ProductName,
    CASE WHEN ni.NutritionalInfoID IS NULL
         THEN 'Missing nutritional info'
         ELSE 'Has nutritional info'
    END AS nutritional_status,
    COALESCE(
        STRING_AGG(DISTINCT ai.AllergenName, ', '),
        'No known allergens'
    ) AS allergens_from_recipe
FROM Product p
LEFT JOIN NutritionalInfo ni ON ni.ProductID = p.ProductID
LEFT JOIN Recipe r ON r.ProductID = p.ProductID
LEFT JOIN AllergyInfo ai ON ai.IngredientID = r.IngredientID
WHERE ni.NutritionalInfoID IS NULL
   OR ai.AllergyInfoID IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY p.ProductID, p.ProductName, ni.NutritionalInfoID
ORDER BY
    CASE WHEN ni.NutritionalInfoID IS NULL THEN 0 ELSE 1 END,
    p.ProductName;

notes.title: Why use LEFT JOIN instead of JOIN?
notes.text: In this bakery context, lEFT JOIN keeps all rows from the left table (Product) even if there is no matching row in the right table. A regular JOIN would exclude bakery products missing nutritional info entirely — the exact bakery products we want to find.

notes.title: What does IS NULL check?
notes.text: In this bakery context, ni.NutritionalInfoID IS NULL identifies bakery products with no matching row in NutritionalInfo. You cannot use = NULL because NULL represents 'unknown,' and any comparison with NULL returns unknown (not true). Always use IS NULL or IS NOT NULL.

notes.title: STRING_AGG with DISTINCT
notes.text: In this bakery context, sTRING_AGG(DISTINCT ai.AllergenName, ', ') concatenates all unique allergen names into a comma-separated string. DISTINCT prevents duplicates if an ingredient appears in multiple recipes.

notes.title: CASE in ORDER BY
notes.text: In this bakery context, cASE WHEN ni.NutritionalInfoID IS NULL THEN 0 ELSE 1 END in ORDER BY sorts bakery products with missing nutritional info first (value 0). This is a computed sort — you are not limited to sorting by column names alone.

checklist: LEFT JOIN for NutritionalInfo, Recipe, and AllergyInfo
IS NULL (not = NULL) to detect missing rows
CASE WHEN IS NULL THEN ... END for conditional label
COALESCE to replace NULL string aggregate with fallback
WHERE uses OR to include missing-nutrition OR has-allergen
CASE in ORDER BY to sort missing items first
businessMeaning: The bakery wants to know which bakery products are missing nutrition or allergy-linked details for compliance.
expectedResultShape: Columns returned are based on the SELECT list for Nutritional & Allergy Compliance.
One row per flagged product showing nutrition status and allergen summary.
oralQuestions: In one sentence, what does Nutritional & Allergy Compliance tell the bakery owner?
Why is each JOIN required for this requirement?

title: Sales by Product Category
desc: Calculate total sales for each product category this quarter.
sql:
SELECT
    pc.CategoryName,
    COUNT(DISTINCT o.OrderID) AS order_count,
    SUM(ol.Quantity) AS total_units_sold,
    SUM(ol.Quantity * ol.UnitPrice) AS total_sales
FROM OrderLine ol
JOIN "Order" o ON o.OrderID = ol.OrderID
JOIN Product p ON p.ProductID = ol.ProductID
JOIN ProductCategory pc ON pc.CategoryID = p.CategoryID
WHERE o.OrderDate >= DATE_TRUNC('quarter', CURRENT_DATE)
  AND o.OrderDate <  DATE_TRUNC('quarter', CURRENT_DATE) + INTERVAL '3 months'
  AND o.Status IN ('Completed','Delivered')
GROUP BY pc.CategoryID, pc.CategoryName
ORDER BY total_sales DESC;

notes.title: Why COUNT(DISTINCT o.OrderID)?
notes.text: In this bakery context, one order can contain multiple order lines (e.g., a latte and a cake). Without DISTINCT, COUNT(o.OrderID) would count each line as a separate order, inflating the number. DISTINCT ensures each order is counted only once.

notes.title: Computed expression in SUM
notes.text: In this bakery context, sUM(ol.Quantity * ol.UnitPrice) multiplies quantity by price for each line and sums the results. The multiplication happens row-by-row before the sum. This calculates actual line-bakery item revenue.

notes.title: Chaining four JOINs
notes.text: In this bakery context, orderLine → Order → Product → ProductCategory. Each JOIN follows a foreign key. The chain lets us filter by order date (from Order) while grouping by category name (from ProductCategory).

checklist: 4-table JOIN chain: OrderLine → Order → Product → ProductCategory
COUNT(DISTINCT o.OrderID) avoids double-counting multi-line orders
Quarter range with DATE_TRUNC + INTERVAL '3 months'
businessMeaning: The bakery wants to know which product categories drive sales this quarter.
expectedResultShape: Columns returned are based on the SELECT list for Sales by Product Category.
One row per product category with orders, units sold, and sales amount.
oralQuestions: In one sentence, what does Sales by Product Category tell the bakery owner?
Why is each JOIN required for this requirement?
What would change if the WHERE filters were removed?
Why is GROUP BY or HAVING needed here (if present)?
What does one output row represent in business terms?

title: Top Customers
desc: Top 5 customers with the highest total spending in the past year.
sql:
SELECT
    c.CustomerID,
    c.FirstName || ' ' || c.LastName AS customer_name,
    c.Email,
    COUNT(DISTINCT o.OrderID) AS total_orders,
    SUM(o.TotalAmount) AS total_spending
FROM Customer c
JOIN "Order" o ON o.CustomerID = c.CustomerID
WHERE o.OrderDate >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '1 year'
  AND o.Status IN ('Completed','Delivered')
GROUP BY c.CustomerID, c.FirstName, c.LastName, c.Email
ORDER BY total_spending DESC
LIMIT 5;

notes.title: What does CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '1 year' mean?
notes.text: In this bakery context, cURRENT_DATE returns today's date. Subtracting INTERVAL '1 year' gives the date exactly one year ago. The WHERE clause uses this to keep only orders placed within the past 12 months.

notes.title: String concatenation with ||
notes.text: In this bakery context, c.FirstName || ' ' || c.LastName concatenates two columns with a space in between. The || operator is PostgreSQL's string concatenation operator. The result is aliased as customer_name.

notes.title: ORDER BY + LIMIT = 'top N' pattern
notes.text: In this bakery context, oRDER BY total_spending DESC LIMIT 5 is the standard SQL pattern for 'top N' queries. Sort all rows by the metric (highest first), then take only the first 5. This is one of the most common patterns in reporting.

checklist: CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '1 year' for rolling 12-month window
ORDER BY total_spending DESC LIMIT 5 (top-N pattern)
businessMeaning: The bakery wants to know which customers spend the most over the last 12 months.
expectedResultShape: Columns returned are based on the SELECT list for Top Customers.
Top 5 customer rows ranked by total spending.
oralQuestions: In one sentence, what does Top Customers tell the bakery owner?
Why is each JOIN required for this requirement?
What would change if the WHERE filters were removed?
Why is GROUP BY or HAVING needed here (if present)?
What does one output row represent in business terms?

Restricts output to a top-N view for quick oral explanation.

title: Best-Selling Products
desc: Top 5 products by sales volume last month.
sql:
SELECT
    p.ProductName,
    pc.CategoryName,
    SUM(ol.Quantity) AS total_quantity_sold,
    SUM(ol.Quantity * ol.UnitPrice) AS total_revenue
FROM OrderLine ol
JOIN "Order" o ON o.OrderID = ol.OrderID
JOIN Product p ON p.ProductID = ol.ProductID
JOIN ProductCategory pc ON pc.CategoryID = p.CategoryID
WHERE o.OrderDate >= DATE_TRUNC('month', CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '1 month')
  AND o.OrderDate <  DATE_TRUNC('month', CURRENT_DATE)
  AND o.Status IN ('Completed','Delivered')
GROUP BY p.ProductID, p.ProductName, pc.CategoryName
ORDER BY total_quantity_sold DESC
LIMIT 5;

notes.title: Half-open date range pattern
notes.text: In this bakery context, >= start AND < end is a 'half-open' range: it includes the start but excludes the end. For monthly ranges this is ideal because DATE_TRUNC gives exact month boundaries. Every timestamp in February falls within >= Feb 1 and < Mar 1.

notes.title: Why GROUP BY includes ProductID?
notes.text: In this bakery context, two bakery products could theoretically share the same name. Grouping by p.ProductID (the primary key) guarantees uniqueness. The name and category are also required in GROUP BY because they appear in SELECT without aggregation.

notes.title: Quantity sold vs. revenue
notes.text: In this bakery context, sUM(ol.Quantity) = how many units moved (volume). SUM(ol.Quantity * ol.UnitPrice) = how much money generated (revenue). A cheap bakery item can be a best-seller by volume but not by revenue.

checklist: SUM(Quantity) for volume, SUM(Quantity * UnitPrice) for revenue
businessMeaning: The bakery wants to know which bakery products sold the most units last month.
expectedResultShape: Columns returned are based on the SELECT list for Best-Selling Products.
Top 5 product rows by quantity sold, with revenue and category.
oralQuestions: In one sentence, what does Best-Selling Products tell the bakery owner?

title: Peak Sales Hours
desc: Which time periods of the day have the most orders?
sql:
SELECT
    CASE
        WHEN EXTRACT(HOUR FROM o.OrderDate) BETWEEN 6 AND 7   THEN '06:00-08:00'
        WHEN EXTRACT(HOUR FROM o.OrderDate) BETWEEN 8 AND 9   THEN '08:00-10:00'
        WHEN EXTRACT(HOUR FROM o.OrderDate) BETWEEN 10 AND 11 THEN '10:00-12:00'
        WHEN EXTRACT(HOUR FROM o.OrderDate) BETWEEN 12 AND 13 THEN '12:00-14:00'
        WHEN EXTRACT(HOUR FROM o.OrderDate) BETWEEN 14 AND 15 THEN '14:00-16:00'
        WHEN EXTRACT(HOUR FROM o.OrderDate) BETWEEN 16 AND 17 THEN '16:00-18:00'
        WHEN EXTRACT(HOUR FROM o.OrderDate) BETWEEN 18 AND 19 THEN '18:00-20:00'
        ELSE 'Other'
    END AS time_range,
    COUNT(o.OrderID) AS order_count,
    SUM(o.TotalAmount) AS total_revenue,
    ROUND(AVG(o.TotalAmount)::numeric, 2) AS avg_order_value
FROM "Order" o
WHERE o.Status IN ('Completed','Delivered')
GROUP BY time_range
ORDER BY order_count DESC;

notes.title: What does EXTRACT(HOUR FROM ...) do?
notes.text: In this bakery context, eXTRACT(HOUR FROM o.OrderDate) pulls out just the hour component (0–23) from a timestamp. For example, 2026-02-14 09:30:00 returns 9. This lets us classify orders into time-of-day buckets.

notes.title: How does CASE create time buckets?
notes.text: In this bakery context, cASE WHEN ... THEN ... END works like an if-else chain. Each WHEN tests a condition; the first true one determines the output. Here it maps hour values to readable time-range labels. ELSE 'Other' catches any hour not explicitly handled.

notes.title: GROUP BY a computed expression
notes.text: In this bakery context, you can GROUP BY time_range even though time_range is a CASE expression. PostgreSQL allows grouping by column aliases in the SELECT list.

notes.title: BETWEEN is inclusive on both sides
notes.text: In this bakery context, bETWEEN 8 AND 9 matches hours 8 and 9. So the bucket '08:00-10:00' captures orders at 8:00–8:59 and 9:00–9:59, which is exactly a 2-hour window.

checklist: CASE WHEN BETWEEN ... THEN ... ELSE 'Other' END for bucketing
BETWEEN is inclusive on both ends
ORDER BY order_count DESC to find peak hours
businessMeaning: The bakery wants to know the busiest order windows to schedule labor and prep.
expectedResultShape: Columns returned are based on the SELECT list for Peak Sales Hours.
One row per time bucket with order count, revenue, and average order value.
oralQuestions: In one sentence, what does Peak Sales Hours tell the bakery owner?
Why is each JOIN required for this requirement?

title: Waste Management Metrics
desc: Top 5 ingredients with the highest waste in the past year.
sql:
SELECT
    i.IngredientName,
    i.UnitOfMeasure AS unit,
    SUM(wl.QuantityWasted) AS total_waste,
    COUNT(wl.WasteLogID) AS waste_incidents,
    STRING_AGG(DISTINCT wl.Reason, '; ') AS reasons
FROM WasteLog wl
JOIN Ingredient i ON i.IngredientID = wl.IngredientID
WHERE wl.WasteDate >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '1 year'
GROUP BY i.IngredientID, i.IngredientName, i.UnitOfMeasure
ORDER BY total_waste DESC
LIMIT 5;

notes.title: What does STRING_AGG do?
notes.text: In this bakery context, sTRING_AGG(DISTINCT wl.Reason, '; ') is an aggregate function that concatenates all distinct reason values within each group into a single semicolon-separated string. DISTINCT removes repeated reasons.

notes.title: Why JOIN (not LEFT JOIN) here?
notes.text: In this bakery context, we only want ingredients that have waste records. An INNER JOIN naturally excludes ingredients with no matching waste log entries. LEFT JOIN would include zero-waste ingredients, which is not useful for a 'top waste' report.

notes.title: COUNT vs SUM for different insights
notes.text: In this bakery context, sUM(wl.QuantityWasted) = total volume wasted. COUNT(wl.WasteLogID) = number of incidents. An ingredient could have high total waste from one large event or from many small ones — both metrics together tell the full story.

checklist: INNER JOIN (not LEFT JOIN) — only ingredients with waste
CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '1 year' for date filter
businessMeaning: The bakery wants to know which ingredients are wasted most so waste can be reduced.
expectedResultShape: Columns returned are based on the SELECT list for Waste Management Metrics.
Top 5 ingredient rows by wasted quantity with incident count and reasons.
oralQuestions: In one sentence, what does Waste Management Metrics tell the bakery owner?
Why is each JOIN required for this requirement?
What would change if the WHERE filters were removed?
Why is GROUP BY or HAVING needed here (if present)?

title: Sales by Channel
desc: Summarize orders and revenue by sales channel last month, with % of total.
sql:
WITH channel_totals AS (
    SELECT
        SUM(TotalAmount) AS grand_total
    FROM "Order"
    WHERE Status IN ('Completed','Delivered')
      AND OrderDate >= DATE_TRUNC('month', CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '1 month')
      AND OrderDate <  DATE_TRUNC('month', CURRENT_DATE)
)
SELECT
    o.SalesChannel AS channel,
    COUNT(o.OrderID) AS number_of_orders,
    SUM(o.TotalAmount) AS sales_revenue,
    ROUND(
        (SUM(o.TotalAmount) / ct.grand_total * 100)::numeric, 1
    ) AS pct_of_total
FROM "Order" o
CROSS JOIN channel_totals ct
WHERE o.Status IN ('Completed','Delivered')
  AND o.OrderDate >= DATE_TRUNC('month', CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '1 month')
  AND o.OrderDate <  DATE_TRUNC('month', CURRENT_DATE)
GROUP BY o.SalesChannel, ct.grand_total
ORDER BY sales_revenue DESC;

notes.title: What does CROSS JOIN do?
notes.text: In this bakery context, cROSS JOIN combines every row from the left table with every row from the right. Here channel_totals is a single row with the grand total. Cross-joining it makes ct.grand_total available on every order row, so each channel can compute its percentage of the whole.

notes.title: Why use a CTE for the grand total?
notes.text: In this bakery context, the channel_totals CTE pre-computes the overall sum once. Without it, you would need a subquery in the SELECT that runs for every row. The CTE approach is clean and efficient.

notes.title: Percentage calculation
notes.text: In this bakery context, (SUM(o.TotalAmount) / ct.grand_total * 100) divides each channel's revenue by the grand total and multiplies by 100 for a percentage. ROUND(..., 1) formats to one decimal place.

notes.title: Why grand_total is in GROUP BY
notes.text: In this bakery context, ct.grand_total appears in SELECT without an aggregate function. SQL requires it to appear in GROUP BY. Since it is the same value for every row (it came from a single-row CTE), grouping by it does not change the grouping behavior.

checklist: CROSS JOIN attaches single-row CTE to every order row
grand_total must appear in GROUP BY (non-aggregated column)
Same date filter applied in both CTE and main query
businessMeaning: The bakery wants to know how revenue splits by in-store, online, and other channels.
expectedResultShape: Columns returned are based on the SELECT list for Sales by Channel.
One row per sales channel with orders, revenue, and percent contribution.
oralQuestions: In one sentence, what does Sales by Channel tell the bakery owner?
Why is each JOIN required for this requirement?

title: Holiday Bestsellers
desc: Most popular bakery products sold during holiday seasons.
sql:
SELECT
    h.HolidayName,
    p.ProductName,
    SUM(ol.Quantity) AS total_sold,
    SUM(ol.Quantity * ol.UnitPrice) AS total_revenue
FROM HolidaySale hs
JOIN Holiday h ON h.HolidayID = hs.HolidayID
JOIN "Order" o ON o.OrderID = hs.OrderID
JOIN OrderLine ol ON ol.OrderID = o.OrderID
JOIN Product p ON p.ProductID = ol.ProductID
WHERE o.Status IN ('Completed','Delivered')
GROUP BY h.HolidayID, h.HolidayName, p.ProductID, p.ProductName
ORDER BY h.HolidayName, total_sold DESC;

notes.title: What is a bridge / associative table?
notes.text: In this bakery context, HolidaySale is an associative table linking orders to holidays so one holiday can include many orders and one order can be tied to holiday analytics.

notes.title: Why group by holiday and product?
notes.text: In this bakery context, grouping by holiday and product gives one row per holiday-product pair so the bakery can compare bestsellers within each season.

notes.title: How ORDER BY is helping
notes.text: In this bakery context, ORDER BY HolidayName then total_sold DESC makes each holiday section easy to explain during oral questioning.

notes.title: Why this JOIN chain matters
notes.text: In this bakery context, each JOIN follows the business path from holiday tags to completed orders to line items to product names; skipping a link changes meaning.

checklist: Explain HolidaySale as the associative table
Trace JOIN path: HolidaySale -> Holiday -> Order -> OrderLine -> Product
Filter to completed or delivered orders only
Interpret total_sold and total_revenue business meaning
Explain dual sort by holiday then bestseller rank
businessMeaning: The bakery wants to know which products perform best during holiday periods for planning stock and marketing.
expectedResultShape: Columns: HolidayName, ProductName, total_sold, total_revenue
One row per holiday-product combination with units sold and revenue.
oralQuestions: In one sentence, what does Holiday Bestsellers tell the bakery owner?
Why is each JOIN required for this requirement?
What would change if the WHERE filters were removed?
Why is GROUP BY or HAVING needed here (if present)?
What does one output row represent in business terms?
